questions.explanation: BIBO stability asks whether every bounded input produces a bounded output. It is a practical input-output notion of stability for systems.

questions.prompt: For a causal LTI system, the output is written as:
questions.explanation: The BIBO proof starts from the convolution integral. That is why knowing the LTI input-output relation is essential before you can prove the boundedness result.

questions.prompt: What does absolute integrability of $h(t)$ mean?
questions.explanation: Absolute integrability requires the total area under the magnitude of the impulse response to be finite. That is the exact condition used in the note to guarantee BIBO stability.

questions.prompt: If the input is bounded by $|x(t)| \le M$, then $|x(t-\tau)|$ is also:
questions.explanation: A bounded-input assumption applies to every time-shifted copy of the same signal. That is why the bound M can be inserted into the convolution inequality.

questions.prompt: Which inequality appears in the BIBO proof?
questions.explanation: This is the key finishing step of the proof. Once the right-hand side is finite, you have shown that the output remains bounded for every bounded input.

questions.prompt: If $h(t)=e^{-t}u(t)$, then the system is BIBO stable because:
questions.explanation: The exponential decays fast enough that the total area under |h(t)| is finite. That satisfies the absolute integrability condition, so the system is BIBO stable.
For h(t)=u(t), the integral of |h(t)| from 0 to infinity becomes the integral of 1, which diverges. That means the impulse response is not absolutely integrable and the BIBO condition fails.

questions.prompt: Why are absolute values necessary in the integrability test?
options: because positive and negative parts could otherwise cancel misleadingly
because h(t) must always be positive
questions.correctAnswer: because positive and negative parts could otherwise cancel misleadingly
questions.explanation: Ordinary integrability could hide a large oscillatory response through cancellation. Absolute integrability prevents that by measuring the true total magnitude that the system contributes in convolution.

questions.prompt: BIBO stability is best described as:
options: a condition that the output must go to zero
a property of the numerator only
questions.explanation: BIBO stability is about what happens to the output when the input remains bounded. It is not the same as demanding that the natural response vanish to zero.

questions.prompt: What is the fastest way to rebuild the BIBO proof if you forget it in the exam?
options: start from convolution, apply absolute values, insert the input bound, and test the remaining integral
differentiate h(t) until it becomes simpler
set the output equal to one
questions.correctAnswer: start from convolution, apply absolute values, insert the input bound, and test the remaining integral
questions.explanation: That is the full logical structure of the proof. It is short, reconstructable, and exactly matches the argument written in the note.
